summary: The author documents their first hands-on experience with Numba, using the Ising model physics simulation as a test case. They implemented a grid-based spin simulation where points flip states based on neighbor interactions and random fluctuations. The Numba-compiled code achieved a 240x speedup (50ms vs 12s) on a 1000x1000 grid. The author emphasizes this was an out-of-the-box experience, not a polished demo, and highlights practical benefits like debuggability and clean separation between data management and computation.
key_points: Numba achieved 240x speedup (12s to 50ms) for Ising model simulation with minimal effort
For-loop-heavy algorithms like Ising model updates are cleaner in imperative code than high-level array operations
Numba allows debugging by simply removing the @jit decorator
Type signatures are optional - Numba will JIT when it sees new signatures with minimal overhead
Data management (NumPy/dask.array) can be cleanly separated from computation (Numba)
money_quotes: I've never actually used Numba. Well that's not quite true; I've indirectly used Numba thousands of times because Blaze auto-generates numba ufuncs.
Numba takes a different approach and translates Python for loops to efficient LLVM code. This is all lower in the hardware stack than where I usually think.
For this kind of algorithm imperative for-loopy code is probably the most clear. You can do this with high-level array operations too (e.g. NumPy/Blaze/Theano), but it's a mess.
This wasn't a canned demo by an expert user / numba developer; this was just my out-of-the-box experience.
I can remove @numba.jit and use the Python debugger
Turns out the cost of not writing it down is that Numba will jit whenever it sees a new signature. For my application this is essentially free.
key_insight: Numba delivers dramatic performance gains for loop-heavy numerical code with minimal learning curve, and its design allows clean separation between data management and computation while preserving debuggability.
